data.title: Vista Energy: My Preferred Oil Stock Due To Key Competitive Advantages Against Big Oil
data.description:
Intro

When investing in oil stocks, the commonly spread logic is to go for the big boys or Supermajors if safety and predictability are key requirements. On top, Big Oil is paying reliable dividends that in most cases are at least kept stable and raised over the long run. At least this is true for the American companies.

I have an opposing view, though. For me, investing in Big Oil makes only sense when there are temporary and short-lived market turbulences, like during the oil price crashes of 2016 and 2020. But else, I view these companies as most of the time fairly valued with little upside. Due to a lack of meaningful organic growth, they are mainly dependent on the movements of oil and gas prices.

Using the case of Vista Energy (NYSE:VIST), I want to explain why it makes more sense to think about considering such small energy growth companies as they have key competitive advantages.

Supermajors have size, well-run small players have different advantages

Vista Energy is a Mexican company, but their main business is mainly the production of unconventional oil (and to a lesser extent also gas, but I'll focus on oil here) from the rapidly growing Vaca Muerta shale field in Argentina.

The following chart shows Vista's assets. In Mexico, 2024 production was only 0.7 Mboe/d while in Argentina, the output was almost 100 times higher at a total of 69 Mboe/d. So it's fair to say that the thesis practically spins around Vaca Muerta in Argentina.

Vista is a fast-growing case. Its management sold the legacy conventional production assets in 2023 to focus solely on its higher-growth and higher-margin shale assets.

Below is a snapshot showing that production volumes, proved reserves and operating earnings (measured by EBITDA) are in a secular growth trend while at the same time management did a terrific job by lowered lifting costs through production efficiencies, but also thanks to having abandoned the higher-cost legacy assets.

Vista's growing operations have led to a phenomenal run of its stock. Noteworthy, shares have resumed their sprint higher, even against falling oil prices.

Over the last twelve months, Vista's stock has run flat. But this is a high achievement in an environment where oil (here measured as Brent oil) during the same period is down 21.5%.

Over five years, the outperformance is even more remarkable.

Part of the impressive run is that Argentina has seen a major revival with the election of its libertarian president. Assets revalued higher from rock-bottom levels. But this is only part of the success story.

The other is that Vista has an excellent founder-management team that under-promises, but over-delivers. Thanks to low production costs of ~30 USD per barrel, the company is able to achieve very competitive margins, even in the currently challenging environment.

Below, you can see that Vista has significantly higher operating margins than industry-behemoths Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX). And not just coincidentally in one year, but constantly.

This proves their lower-cost business model. Thanks to higher earnings and cash flows, Vista is able to invest full-throttle back into its business for growth. This is the reason why free cash flow at first sight looks rather weak. But strong sales and EBITDA growth, as well as production volumes prove this wheel is spinning full-speed. Cash conversion (EBITDA --> operating cash flow) is strong.

Practically the entire operating cash flow is pumped back into drilling and production activities.

Management is able to convince with their execution. The current goal is to achieve a production run-rate of 150 Mboe/d. Until recently, this goal was until around 2030.

However, this goal has aged quickly. Not only is Vista constantly beating their guidance, but they also just announced a truly transformative deal. Vista has already bought post the Q1 earnings period in a 1.5 billion USD cash and stock deal some neighboring Vaca Muerta assets. The timing could not have been better than now during uncertainty and low-energy prices where assets are attractively valued. The energy sector is rather famous for value-destructive deals when prices are high.

That this will likely prove to be a major acquisition for Vista can be seen not only by the fact that the company will achieve its production goals significantly quicker. Management during the last conference call already said indirectly that they'll raise their guidance soon and that there will be a capital markets day later this year with an update.

The other thing is, the economics of the scooped up assets are very favorable.

The acquired assets are shared with YPF (YPF), the big state-owned competitor. On a working-interest basis (50%), Vista will add approximately 40 Mboe/d or increase their current production volumes by almost 50%. At the same time, they will have added 140 million barrels of proved reserves to their own 375 million barrels as per FY 2024 (+37%). Likewise, the deal brings critical mid-stream assets with it so that Vista has better and cheaper transportation capabilities.

Especially last year, Vista outpaced its own transportation capabilities and had to rely on more expensive trucking. A pipeline to Chile was in expansion and is now complete. According to the CEO, Vista is not using trucking anymore, but has the flexibility to do so every time the need arises.

The following chart proves that this deal very likely is highly accretive. The valuation paid for these assets was significantly below Vista's multiples on several metrics. This is important as part of the deal is also a smaller equity component.

With this overview, it is clear that Vista has certain key advantages compared to Big Oil: it grows organically at a fast speed, making it much less dependent on favorable energy prices as the major tailwind. As shown with the charts above, Vista's stock can even pace forward in times of falling oil prices.

Vista also has much better economics, i.e. lower production costs and thus higher margins. The generated cash is being aggressively reinvested into the business for more growth, spinning a powerful wheel.

What many investors will not necessarily agree to, but for me, it is a clear advantage: Vista has no burden to pay a dividend. While income investors have other motivations than growth investors, this lack for me is a tailwind, as Vista does not have to make sure to keep a long dividend series intact, if necessary even at the expense of the balance sheet. I am mentioning this, as during 2020 ExxonMobil and Chevron took on plenty of debt to defend their dividends. I understand the motivation behind this move, but it was not necessarily in the best interest of shareholders from the perspective of maximizing shareholder value. If at all, buybacks would have made more sense, but their "mandate" to keep dividends at least stable is limiting the flexibility I like to see.

Valuation

Vista Energy per last count (FY 2024) generated operating cash flow of 960 million USD. This number was a bit suppressed due to unfavorable working capital movements. With the freshly acquired assets, this figure will grow noticeably, likely more towards 1.5 billion USD. As shown in the presentation slides above, the EBITDA guidance is 1.7 billion USD. From the tikr-charts we have seen that operating cash flow is trailing EBITDA reliably and closely, thus 1.5 billion USD in operating cash flow is a good starting point – even at the currently undemanding oil prices!

The market cap is currently 5.2 billion USD while net debt was around 800 million USD per end of March (Q1 2025 results) for an enterprise value of 6 billion USD. The Q2 results will then include the closed acquisition and a modestly higher share count. But the multiple will be somewhere in the mid single digits only – for a high-quality growth story!

Based on FY 2024 figures, Exxon and Chevron both trade at more than 8x operating cash flow, just for comparison. For practically no organic growth. While they have a valuation premium thanks to their size, asset diversification, prominence and dividend, as laid out, I view Vista Energy as a (much) better choice with strong advantages that both Big Boys do not have. Much higher growth at a lower multiple with time will likely result in much better performance.

Risks to my thesis

The two biggest risks I am seeing are the volatility of the oil price and political risks. Like its competitors, Vista cannot influence the oil price. If oil falls due to higher recession fears, sales and earnings will take a meaningful hit, negatively affecting the share price. The other point is, Argentina is currently experiencing positive tailwinds from the political side from deregulations, e.g. regarding currency and export controls, but also international price parity (pushing back domestic subsidies). Should the political wind shift again, Vista's growth story could be in jeopardy.

If any of these work against the business, the stock will likely not be able to withstand.

Conclusion

Vista Energy is one of my best ideas to play the energy sector. A company with key competitive advantages like low production costs, strong margins, high growth rates and a very capable management as stressed with their recent transformative deal, make this a clever pick, especially compared to Big Oil.

data.title: Cloudflare: Investing To Grow
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice, and nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

Dig a Little Deeper

By Hermit Warrior

Scrolling through Cloudflare's (NET) Q1 Investor presentation, you would think nothing's really changed from the last earnings presentation - I even checked to see if it wasn't in fact the previous quarter's. Nothing new - or very interesting - to see here.

The March 12th Investor Day Presentation, on the other hand, is a different story (at least the second half). Here we get to see what's really going on under the hood, with interesting insights on all sorts of areas of the business. More on that in a bit.

The headlines paint a picture of consistent revenue growth, at around 27% year-on-year (beating the guide which was for just under 24%) and guiding for 25% growth in Q3 which, if they beat by the same margin as this quarter, would, in fact, mean growth accelerates a touch (something that hasn't happened since Q4 '21).

EBITDA margins ticked up, as did net cash. For the full year, the guide is for 25% revenue growth. All in all, pretty much what we've come to expect from NET.

Now, back to that Investor Day presentation; that's where things get a little more interesting. Let's take a look.

For starters, NET is stepping up their investments in the business, expanding into new geographies and - as per the above slide (which for some reason is not the same as the version in the Q1 Investor Presentation; this one's way more interesting) - doubling their compute capacity, made up of cloud-based CPU, GPU and storage resources. This is reflected in surging capex spend, which at over $92M in the quarter, more than doubled YoY.

Next - a big, and growing focus on the developer community, now numbering over 3 million, from 450,000 in mid-2022 - and compared to 6 million for NVIDIA (NVDA), a much larger enterprise - and the source of a large $100M contract (their largest ever) highlighted in the Q1 results.

And finally - and most importantly from the perspective of what will ultimately move the share price - an improving Go-to-Market outlook with sales productivity up, more reps achieving sales quotas, and a plan to hire more Enterprise focused reps - with mention in one of the slides of an aspirational $5 billion in revenues.

Large customers (greater than $100K in annual revenues) now represent 69% of total revenues, from 65% in Q4, and there is an increasing focus on acquiring even larger customers - those with over $1M+ in annual revenues - with NET going so far as to comparing their own $1M customer count to a range of peers at different stages of revenue growth (Cloudflare also serves millions of 'customers' through free accounts that provide basic caching and security for small web-sites).

This from Matthew Prince, CEO, from the Q1 earnings call transcript:

We saw particular strength with our largest customers, those that spend over $1 million and $5 million with Cloudflare per year. Within both of these cohorts, we added a record number of customers, increasing 48% and 54% year-over-year, respectively...

This focus bodes well for future revenue growth, especially as they scale where only larger deals will have the potential to 'move the needle'.

Of note, there is an interesting series of slides in the Investor Day presentation (start at slide 38) articulating Cloudflare's vision of the evolution - and importance - of AI within the process of building applications, and how these are transforming outcomes from augmenting worker productivity, to automating key functions. This is very similar to how SentinelOne (S) has set out their vision of AI.

Let's move on to fundamentals, valuation and technicals.

Financial Fundamentals

It's very likely that the increased focus on larger customers has allowed revenue growth to remain stable, and potentially sets the scene - as highlighted earlier - for some acceleration, as opposed to the gentle decline of the last several years, should they manage to beat their Q2 guide by a similar margin as in Q1.

Note the large capex spend this quarter, even higher than last quarter's 17% of revenues. It also looks like there will be a continued focus on heavy R&D spend with the long-term model targeting 18-20% of revenue.

Remaining Performance Obligations [RPO] growth accelerated year-on-year, to 39% (a positive indicator for sustained/improving revenue growth) and now sits at around 105% of annual revenues.

Valuation Metrics

Valuation remains punchy at 30x EV/TTM revenues, but lower than after Q4 earnings when it stood at 35x - still, pretty good for a business that recorded a GAAP operating loss of 11% of revenues, and only $2 million of unlevered free cashflow [UFCF] in the quarter. The market continues to be very forgiving of NET for these 'transgressions' across the more traditional valuation metrics.

In fact, the share price has surged by over 25% since the May 8th earnings release, compared to around 7.5% for Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and a similar performance for Zscaler (ZS) in the same period. Interestingly, NET's share price was also up 23% after Q4 earnings. It's worth noting that NET has traded at around 60x TTM revenues (2021-2022) although those seem like different times.

Also of note, in my mind at least, is how NET has not been punished for trying to be 'everything to everyone' (and having all those free, resource-hungry accounts); it provides wide area network (WAN) services - connecting through their extensive cloud - network and application security, content delivery services (accelerating applications and content delivery to end users) and a Developer Platform for the creation of cloud-native applications.

With under $2 billion in annual revenues, NET is not particularly large as tech players go. Supporting such a broad portfolio likely helps to explain the relatively high capex at 19% of quarterly revenue, compared to, for example, 9% at CRWD. Happily for shareholders, the market is not focused on this for now.

Stock Charts

Our preferred technical analysis method involves Elliott Waves and Fibonacci levels, and on the short-term chart, it looks like NET is reaching the top of a local Wave 5, with the large cluster of high-volume nodes and sideway-ish price action at the top of the chart indicating possible short-term distribution. You can reach the short-term chart here.

The medium term chart shows a near-vertical recovery from the April lows with what looks like a local Wave (ii) about to start, as it continues to climb up the larger degree Wave 3. You can reach the medium term chart here.

As with so many stocks following the strong rally of the last few weeks, NET seems - in my view at least - poised to retrace some of its recent gains as it prepares for the next leg up. If I were looking to start a new position, or add to an existing one, I would wait for the market to offer a better entry point, perhaps around the $124-$128 mark.

Longer term, the larger degree Wave 3 - supported by the promising growth profile and fundamentals - could take the price to the $265 mark, and beyond.

We rate NET at Hold.

Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 21 May 2025.

data.title: Principal Well-Being Index: Small Businesses Are Holding On To Their Staff
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist and Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Despite a drop in business sentiment, measured by the Principal Well-Being Index, small and medium-sized businesses are holding on to their staff, likely due to the memory of post-COVID difficulty in rehiring.

Such a preference for labor hoarding comes even before the U.S.-China trade truce, indicating that small business layoffs may be even less likely in the months ahead. This should help prevent unemployment from rising significantly this year, which, in turn, lowers recession risk.

According to the Principal Well-Being Index, small and medium-sized businesses - despite a sharp plunge in sentiment - are planning to hold on to their staff, likely due to the memory of post-COVID difficulty in rehiring.

The Principal Well-Being Index tracks sentiment across small and medium-sized businesses by asking a set of eight questions about current conditions and the economic outlook. Now in its 12th year, the latest survey was fielded amid peak trade policy uncertainty from April 14 to 25. It gathered responses from 1,000 businesses - comparable in scale to the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index.

Survey results revealed that business confidence has fallen to the second-lowest level in the survey’s history, mainly due to concerns over rising costs and tariff-driven uncertainty.

Survey results also revealed, however, that many businesses continue to favor labor hoarding over layoffs, and employers instead are doubling down on implementing operational efficiencies and cost-cutting strategies to manage risk.

With peak economic uncertainty likely passed, and despite small businesses - employing nearly half the U.S. private workforce - bearing the brunt of economic uncertainty, the risk of widespread small business layoffs appears to be receding. Therefore, while the economy is set to slow in the months ahead, unemployment is unlikely to rise significantly, and this suggests that the broader economy is well-positioned to weather increased policy headwinds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line: Discounted Stock With Durable Growth
data.description:
Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH) shares are down more than 31% year-to-date on the back of the macroeconomic backdrop. However, in its most recent earnings release, the company reaffirmed its full-year guidance, despite the earnings miss and warning for softer consumer demand for more premium cruises. In fact, the business continues to operate profitably and is expanding with a disciplined pipeline of new ships. While Wall Street remains focused on Norwegian’s lagging stock price compared to Royal Caribbean (RCL) and Carnival (CCL), I see a setup where expectations are far too low. With a pipeline of new ships, easing consumer sentiment pressure, and clear margin upside from network optimization, I think the firm may be one of the most attractive opportunities in the entire leisure space for long-term investors.

Solid Execution Despite Volume Headwinds

In my opinion, Norwegian Cruise Line's Q1 of 2025 shows a company that knows how to do the basics right. Most importantly, things such as managing yields, controlling costs, and hitting its financial targets, even if the revenue may have come a bit short compared to last year.

Unfortunately, the first quarter revenue came in lower at $2.13 billion, or 3% less than 12 months ago, but somehow this was expected given that management was projecting a drop in occupancy to 101.5% from 104.6% last year, due to the increased drydock days repositioning itineraries, especially for ships like Norwegian Breakaway and Bliss.

Yet, Norwegian also exceeded expectations where it matters. Net yield grew 1.2% year-over-year on a constant currency basis, beating the company’s own forecast of 0.5% and showcasing the solid business environment. But if we have to look at the peers, that result was nowhere near its competitors. For context, Royal Caribbean reported net yield growth of 5.6%, while Carnival surged 7.3%.

In an environment with consistently rising input costs, adjusted net cruise costs excluding fuel increased by just 3%, and would have been only 1.2% if we excluded the $8 impact from drydocks. Such a level of cost control surely makes the business stand out, but cost control was simply not enough to improve the bottom line.

The adjusted EBITDA actually declined by 2% to $453 million compared to $464 million at the same time last year. Maybe management has given a rather conservative number at the end of fiscal 2024, given that despite the weakness on more premium cruises, the number still came above the guidance of $435 million. It is possible that the company was predicting a slightly worse outcome.

Only Adjusted EPS came slightly below the firm's estimates at $0.07 compared to the initial expectations of $0.08 a share. However, this was due to $0.05 of negative foreign exchange impact.

Overall, despite the challenges faced by the business, there are some positive signs of growth and resilience in the face of adversity. With some of the strategic adjustments the company is planning to make and its focus on improving key metrics, I don't see a reason why the business cannot continue its success in the future.

The Disciplined Gameplan Begins to Show Results

Much of Norwegian’s momentum heading into 2025 can be traced back to the progress made under its “Charting the Course” strategy that was introduced in April last year. The initiative aims to sharpen operational discipline while enhancing the guest experience.

A centerpiece of that strategy is going to be the fleet optimization. The upcoming debut of the Norwegian Aqua, the first of the Prima Plus class, shows a smarter approach to capital allocation. Though the vessel is 10% larger than its predecessors, it fits more staterooms and high-margin attractions into the same footprint by shedding bulkier, low-ROI amenities like go-kart tracks. Instead, experiences like the new Aqua coaster have become a huge hit online and have already generated over 270 million media impressions, according to management.

At the same time, Norwegian is quietly modernizing and rationalizing its fleet. The company recently signed agreements to charter out four vessels, Norwegian Sky, Sun, Navigator, and Insignia, to third-party operators in India for residential cruising. These moves will free up resources, reduce fleet age, and lower the complexity of operations, all while monetizing underperforming assets.

This newfound opportunity probably comes at the right moment. Norwegian is currently preparing for a major fleet expansion, an ambitious but well-calculated move. Twelve new ships are on order as of now, with Allura set to become the second ship launched in July. The plan aims to deliver a 4% CAGR in gross capacity through 2028 and a 3% CAGR through 2036, effectively locking in growth for more than a decade.

I don't think it is just the capacity expansion that strengthens this plan, but also the margin focus that underpins it. Guest satisfaction can be hard to quantify, but the company’s intent to improve profitability is crystal clear. Norwegian now has an 86% increase in advanced ticket sales compared to Q1 of 2019, and volumes are holding steady versus last quarter, which shows to me that the demand remains strong.

Technology, on the other hand, is also starting to play an even bigger role in business. The newly launched NCL app, fully rolled out in Q1, has already attracted over 800,000 users and is helping to lift pre-sold onboard spending. More importantly, it gives Norwegian a scalable platform to personalize offers and improve guest monetization, something that Royal Caribbean has mastered, and Norwegian now appears eager to replicate for themselves.

Put together, these developments paint a constructive picture for 2025. Despite the minor drop in revenue and capacity, management reaffirmed its full-year guidance, including net yield growth of 2% to 3%, occupancy averaging 102.5%, and adjusted EPS of $2.05, which is a 12.6% improvement over FY2024.

Importantly, cost expectations have also improved. The company now anticipates net cruise cost growth of just 0% to 1.25% for the year, down from earlier forecasts, with $300 million in transformation savings being pulled forward to support flexibility if revenue trends soften.

In other words, while the first half of the year may not be the best, Norwegian’s full-year setup still looks promising.

One of the Cheapest Ways to Play Cruising

Norwegian Cruise shares have experienced a significant decline, but mainly since the beginning of February this year. The stock is down over 32% year-to-date, but still holding up 3% on an annual basis. Shares of the company are currently trading at $17.5 a piece, a sharp contrast compared to the $29 levels the company was flirting with less than 4 months ago.

If we look at the chart below, we can see that the 3 major cruise liners were moving together. However, since the beginning of February, the market has started to differentiate between them, and as a result, RCL outperforms NCLH by as much as 56% on a 12-month basis.

Analysts are still optimistic, with an average price target of $24.82, which points to more than 40% upside from current levels. That said, expectations have come down quite a bit since the start of 2025. The agreement among the market was for a target price north of $30 just 2 months ago. Nevertheless, there could still be a big opportunity if we assume that the stock has been brought down by the market rather than specific business issues. If so, I think those 3 metrics can prove it.

Currently, Norwegian Cruise trades at a notable discount when we compare the business to its peers. For instance, the forward P/E ratio the business is trading in is 8.9x, way cheaper than Carnival's 12.3x or Royal Caribbean's 16.3x net earnings.

Meanwhile, it’s Norwegian’s Price/Cash Flow that really makes the company stand out, coming in at just 4.2, versus 12.1 for Royal Caribbean and 5.9 for Carnival.

On an EV/EBITDA basis, Norwegian again seems rather humble, with 8.4x forward EBITDA, in the same ballpark as Carnival's 8.5x but way less than the 12.9x Royal Caribbean is trading at.

These metrics suggest that Norwegian Cruise may be undervalued compared to its main competitors and make the business an interesting proposal for investors who look for a cruise liner at a bargain. Additionally, these metrics are also lower than the sector median.

However, before you do, let's get familiar with the risks, too.

Risks: Tariffs, Confidence, and Leverage

There’s no denying that 2024 was a strong year for Norwegian, but the momentum started fading toward the end, and this is what is concerning. Back in Q1 of last year, revenue was growing at a healthy 20% pace. By Q4, that had slowed to just 6%, and now in the latest quarter, revenue actually declined by 3%. Looking ahead, analysts expect only about 6.5% growth for the full year, down noticeably from the 11% the company posted in 2024. That’s a clear sign that the pace is cooling.

Share prices took a massive hit as a result of Trump's tariff announcement. Although this has no direct impact on the company, the potential decrease in consumer confidence can have an actual material impact on the cruise line industry. Let's not forget that consumer confidence has already declined for a fifth consecutive month, and according to the data, the last time consumer confidence was at such lows was in 2020. Any further deterioration could result in a potential decline in pre-books, which most certainly will impact the capacity rate and onboard spending.

And last but not least, we have to acknowledge the slightly higher debt the business is carrying. To put it into perspective, Norwegian has a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.7x, which is higher than the likes of RCL or CCL. Even though this ratio is increased in Q1 with the arrival of the new ship, the strong cash-flow generation should be able to comfortably reduce the ratio to around 5x by the end of the year, but it will take a few more years before it goes below 3x.

Conclusion

Norwegian Cruise is demonstrating a solid financial performance, and the business is expected to grow steadily over the next decade, driven by the strong demand for travel and a pipeline of ships. While NCLH's share price has declined significantly more than its peers, probably due to the extra debt the company carries, I am not really concerned. As it is already being paid off at a rapid rate without taking a toll on growth. Therefore, I believe the current share price offers a compelling upside opportunity for the long term.

data.title: NovoCure: Loading Up On Growth Catalysts
data.description:
It has been roughly five months since my previous NovoCure (NVCR) article, where I discussed the company’s PANOVA-3 data in pancreatic cancer, and my game plan to add to my NVCR position if the share price was able to break out and hold above $32.50. Well, the ticker has had a bearish rating on the Go-No-Go indicator since January 24th and is down roughly 40% since that article. I have been eyeing NVCR over the past few months, but the company’s big miss on their Q4 EPS raised some concerns about the company’s ability to maintain their commercial efforts, while still funding an impressive clinical pipeline. Thankfully, the company recently reported their Q1 earnings with a beat on both EPS and revenue, which appears to have injected some bullish energy into the share price. I have yet to touch my NVCR position in 2025, but the encouraging earnings and long list of growth catalysts have forced me to keep a close eye on the ticker as we head towards the second half.

I intend to review some of the company’s pending catalysts and how they could yield significant growth in the coming years. Then, I will point out some downside risks that investors should consider when managing their NVCR position. Finally, I take a look at the NVCR Daily Chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to add my position.

NSCLC Inflection

NovoCure’s launch of their Optune Lua Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields) device for metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is perhaps their most important event since their Optune Gio glioblastoma [GBM] approval. The company reported Optune Lua pulled in $1.5M in Q1, with $7OOK coming from metastatic NSCLC with $800K coming from malignant pleural mesothelioma [MPM]. Indeed, that revenue number is not jaw-dropping, but the company reported that 92 U.S. prescriptions were written in Q1, which was up from 52 in Q4. NovoCure revealed that 60% of their new Q1 prescribers are just starting out using tumor treating fields therapy, with “many come from institutions that have never prescribed Optune Gio for GBM.” In addition, NovoCure got the CE Mark approval in Europe, so the NSCLC indication appears to be gathering momentum as we look to the back half of 2025.

The combination of platinum chemotherapy and a checkpoint inhibitor as a first-line treatment is becoming more common, which aligns with Optune Lua’s on-label population. In the company’s Q1 earnings call, they discussed early adoption by three primary patient groups. The first is patients who responded to first-line platinum chemo and checkpoint, but are now showing early signs of relapse. The second group has progressed rapidly despite first-line chemo and checkpoint, and are now onto second-line options. The third group is low or non-PD-L1 expressors who started on chemo as a mono, but are looking for another option to just prolonged chemotherapy. Together, these groups form a ~30K eligible patient U.S. market annually for Optune Lua.

Promisingly, early adoption has been approximately a 50-50 split between those receiving Optune Lua in combination with a checkpoint inhibitor and those using Optune Lua while using docetaxel (second-line taxane-based chemo). What is more, roughly 50% of prescriptions were for second-line and another 25% for the third-line. This is encouraging because it indicates that physicians are acting quickly by starting Optune Lua at the first signs of progression, and are not employing it as a salvage therapy.

So, it looks as if Optune Lua is being deployed to support the best clinical and commercial outcomes. Therefore, the Optune Lua launch could actually generate growth metrics that could appease the Street.

Catalysts in Play

Looking ahead, NovoCure has multiple catalysts in play for the remainder of 2025. Most notably, the company remains on track for PMA submission for their METIS and PANOVA-3 trials.

For the near-term, I am looking at the PANOVA-3 data that is set to be presented on the main stage at ASCO, which will put TTF data right in front of target physicians and key opinion leaders. It looks as if this is going to be a full dataset from the Phase III trial in locally advanced pancreatic cancer, so we should see the first publicized data on comprehensive demographic information, Kaplan-Meier survival curves, and some extra details on secondary endpoints.

The study did achieve a meaningful two-month survival benefit, which is an incredibly rare outcome in this setting, so the trial was a success… however, it is possible that ASCO data could make PANOVA-3 one of the most important clinical trials ever to be completed for pancreatic cancer.

Perhaps a more underrated catalyst is that the NCCN guidelines for lung cancer are under review in July. The NCCN guidelines are the “gold standard” for the clinical practice for NSCLC, so Optune Lua’s inclusion will be critical for adoption and payer support.

International Growth

NovoCure also has substantial growth potential outside the U.S. thanks to their recent CE Mark approval for Optune Lua. The European label is similar to the U.S., which will allow providers to use the device in combination with checkpoint inhibitors or docetaxel. The company revealed that Germany is their immediate focus for launch, where they have a trained commercial team ready to go for NSCLC. The company expects a case-by-case reimbursement model at first, but they also have expectations for broader access by year-end.

Overall, the company is seeing strong demand numbers outside the U.S., including in France, where there was a 46% year-over-year increase in active patients in Q1, and Japan logging 17% growth. The company does expect the growth rate from France to cool off, but it is clear that the demand is there, and that country can deliver a respectable amount of revenue in the coming years for both GBM and NSCLC.

Tracking Growth

NovoCure has been recording considerable growth since launching Optune Gio and Optune Lua in multiple markets. I was particularly encouraged by $155M in revenue in Q1, which was up 12% year-over-year. I will point out that the increase did not come at an oversized increase in expenses. The company also finished the quarter with ~$929M in cash and investments, which should provide them with enough runway to keep the pipeline moving while scaling.

The Street is expecting NovoCure to report single-digit growth over the next couple of years, however, it looks like they are anticipating an inflection to double-digit growth in 2027.

For the EPS, the Street analysts are forecasting a drop for both 2025 and 2026. Then, strong double-digit growth in 2027, 2028, 2029, and 2030.

Can They Hit These Numbers?

There is no argument that NovoCure's TTF technology puts them in a strong position to potentially disrupt and claim a beachhead in oncology markets that could hit $70B by 2030.

When I look at the market sizes, the potential is massive. The NSCLC market alone could reach $50B by 2030, with pancreatic possibly climbing above $10B by 2034, and GBM being forecasted to be ~$5.7B by 2033. Plus, brain metastases remain a serious unmet need for up to 25% of cancer patients, and NovoCure is one of the few companies offering a non-systemic treatment option that has the potential to be used throughout the treatment plan. I’m not suggesting that every TTFields device will necessarily become a blockbuster, but the size of the addressable markets provides NovoCure with a real opportunity to meet or even exceed the Street’s expectations. With strong commercial momentum, a growing portfolio of devices, and continued international expansion, the conditions are nearly perfect for growth.

Risks to Consider

Despite my bullish outlook, NovoCure has several risks that investors need to consider when managing their position. First, I would point out the execution risks and commercial hurdles. It is possible that Medicare reimbursement could take 12+ months for NSCLC patients using Optune Lua. There are also tariff-related costs and inflation that could impact the company’s margins by up to $11M in 2025. The company also noted that their trial enrollments for LUNAR-2 and LUNAR-4 are still in the early stages. Admittedly, these are just speed bumps at the moment, however, they can also push some timelines back, as well as impact the company’s quarterly performance.

In addition to the newly outlined risks, I must reiterate one of my previous concerns. The labels are dependent on chemo backbones, which could be a long-term issue if the cancer treatment paradigm shifts in the coming decade. Of course, there is competition and the possibility the company will have to defend their IP at some point… all of which will put some stress on the company’s cash position of $929.1M. Indeed, the company is far from running out of cash at the moment, but their net income is still deep in the red, and their some volatility in their cash flow statements. Therefore, investors need to keep the company’s finances as a lingering risk for the foreseeable future.

Still, I am maintaining my NVCR conviction rating of 4 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio.

My Plan

My NVCR game plan remains the same… I am not willing to pull my position out of mothballs until I see strong confirmation that the ticker's response to the pancreatic cancer data is durable, which it was not. So, I am going to wait to see what happens after the PANOVA-3 presentation at ASCO (May 31st) before making a move.

If the data is strong and the market responds appropriately, I will look to pull the trigger on a buy order if the share price can break and hold above that downtrend ray from the December 2024 high. After that, I will look to make periodic additions to my NVCR position as long as the ticker remains above the anchored VWAP that was from the June 2023 sell-off, and the December 2024 High (~$21-$22 per share).

I am still willing to make NVCR one of my largest positions in the "Bio Boom" speculative portfolio, however, I need to see the market share with the same conviction. So, I will have to make adjustments as we move through some of the pending catalysts for 2025 and 2026.

Overall, my goal is to trade NVCR in order to generate profits, while generating a "House Money" core position to hold for a long-term position.

data.title: A Stock Picker's Guide To Volatility: Global Equity Market Opportunities Across A Shifting Landscape
data.description:
Episode Description:

It's been quite a ride for equity markets this year. Uncertainty and volatility hit a fever pitch in the weeks following President Trump's sweeping tariff announcement in April as investors feared the potential for rising inflation, slow economic growth, and US assets losing their aura of dominance. Tony DeSpirito, global CIO for BlackRock's Fundamental Equities Group, joins host Oscar Pulido to help make sense of recent market turmoil from an equity perspective where he sees opportunities amidst the volatility and what history tells us to expect as we look ahead to the rest of the year.

Transcript

Oscar Pulido: It's been quite a ride for equity markets this year. Uncertainty and volatility hit a fever pitch in the weeks following President Trump's sweeping tariff announcement in April as investors feared the potential for rising inflation, slow economic growth, and US assets losing their aura of dominance.

Welcome to The Bid, where we break down what's happening in the markets and explore the forces changing the economy and finance. I'm Oscar Pulido. Coming up, Tony DeSpirito, global CIO for BlackRock's Fundamental Equities Group, joins me to help make sense of recent market turmoil from an equity perspective where he sees opportunities amidst the volatility and what history tells us to expect as we look ahead to the rest of the year.

Tony, thank you so much for joining us on The Bid.

Tony DeSpirito: It's great to be here again, Oscar.

Oscar Pulido: And Tony, you were here in January. We were talking about your outlook on the stock market for the year. Animal spirits were high. People were optimistic on the economy. I remember you saying that stocks had been up more than 20% for consecutive years and that it was rare for it to happen three years in a row. And it looks like your viewpoint on that is correct because we've had a lot of volatility this year. We've had announcements around tariffs that caused stocks to pull back. We've now seen a rally that has seemed to get us back to where we were at the beginning of the year. So tell us a little bit about what you're observing and what's going to cause markets to stabilize.

Tony DeSpirito: Sure. Without a doubt, there was a lot of optimism at the beginning of the year, and that's how you get two years of 20% plus in a row. Optimism, and there's a decent amount to be optimistic about. Consumer balance sheets were really strong going into the year, still are, and that's result of both strong house prices as well as financial assets. And of course, that provides a good macro backdrop for the markets because there's a lot of cash on the sidelines waiting to get in, right? So, that's always a good thing. And then corporate balance sheets are in pretty good shape as well. So, no real big excesses there.

And then you look at earnings, earnings growth is still quite healthy, so that's the positives. The only concern I would've had at the beginning of the year was really around valuation. Valuations are high by historical standards, and that was certainly true at the beginning of the year. And then we've gotten a lot of uncertainty around policy. So, if you look at any kind of policy uncertainty index, that's been off the charts this year, and it's all related to trade. And so, we had a market drawdown. And if you look at peak to trough, that was about 17%. But if you think about what's happened subsequently is we had peak uncertainty.

Since then, we've had a number of trade deals get struck and a number more in progress, and so uncertainty has come down. And with it, the market has recovered. And we've been saying, stick with equities, don't exit the market, stay with your strategy. And the reason behind that is it doesn't stay uncertain forever. And once you have certainty, almost no matter what the certainty is, it's good for equities. In the sense that corporations can then adapt themselves to that, and then you see earnings go up and stock prices go up with it.

And that's what we've seen subsequently is that the markets have generally recovered. So, if you look point to point, it almost looked like nothing happened, but obviously, there's been a lot of volatility in between.

Oscar Pulido: And when we zoom out, many years from now and look at these few months, it will look like nothing happened. But of course, the day-to-day has felt very different. And Tony, when you and I talk, one of the things that I always find interesting is that you have these historical anecdotes. You're able to go back and look at some data points from the past and tie it to what's going on in markets today. So, what does history tell us about the recent volatility in the market events that have been going on?

Tony DeSpirito: Yeah, so history's pretty clear on this, actually. if you look over the last, call it 35 years, go back to 1990 and look at the average drawdown in any given year, it averages roughly 14%. So, this year we had a 17%, drawdown peak to trough. I'd say it's right in line with historical averages. So really from the historical scheme of things, no big deal. It obviously felt like a big deal, but it really wasn't when you look at it from a historical point of view.

The other interesting thing is, if you look historically, buying dips has been pretty profitable. In fact, we went back all the way to, the 1930s and looked at data. What happens when you buy on a dip of 20% or more? We use 20% as the cutoff. And it's pretty interesting. On average, if you look one year later, so if you buy it exactly the 20% dip, even if it goes lower, one year later, on average, you have returns of 15% positive. So that's healthy relative to historical averages. What's really interesting is the hit rate. 73% of the time, the returns were positive one year later. And if you extend that time horizon out to three years, you actually get to over 90% hit rate on positive returns. And so, I think that tells you in general, you want to have a bias towards buying the dips.

Now, when might you be more hesitant to buy the dips? Historically, if you look back, it's when there have been big dislocations in the economy. Think about the global financial crisis, where consumer balance sheets were extremely levered. That's an example of where you might want to be more hesitant in buying the dips, where the downturn can be bigger and more protracted. But again, go back to what we experienced this year, consumer balance sheets are healthy. Corporate balance sheets are healthy. I think everything told you this would be a good, dip buying opportunity.

Oscar Pulido: And it's fascinating. You mentioned going back and looking at the 20% pullbacks in markets, which is higher than a historical average. But, when we hit 20% drawdowns, the media and the press and the narrative starts to talk about how we're in a bear market, 20% is usually when we denote that as a bear market. So, it requires a certain level of courage to be able to invest at that time. But what you're saying is that it has been rewarded if you've come in at that time period.

Tony DeSpirito: Exactly. And that's why we chose the 20% because it's what commonly people think of as the 'bear market'. 10% is a correction, 20% is a bear market. And so that's why we use that particular marker.

Oscar Pulido: Tony, you recently published an equity market outlook for the second quarter of the year. I just want to read a couple of things that you wrote in there, which is volatility can present great opportunity, and dispersion is an active investor's friend. So, talk to us a little bit about where are you finding the opportunities right now as an active investor?

Tony DeSpirito: Yeah, it's an important point that I want to emphasize because our job as active investors is really to take advantage of dispersion, to take advantage of volatility. That's what we do. And I think of active returns is a function of both skill set and the opportunity set. And the way I measure the opportunity set is volatility and dispersion. In fact, I think the hardest time for active managers is when there's very limited volatility, very limited dispersion.

So, I think really that period after the Global Financial Crisis from the end of the global financial crisis through to Covid was a period of actually very low volatility. The market went up quite nicely, actually. Returns were about double historical averages with less volatility. So, there was an easy answer, just buy cheap beta. But since Covid, markets have been more volatile, and that's actually made things much more interesting for active investors. So, obviously, we've had a lot of uncertainty around trade and trade policy, so I think that's been certainly an area.

As I look forward, I think there's more policy to come. Tax, for example, that'll present some winners and losers, and that'll create opportunities for active managers. Deregulation, I think that's a real opportunity, particularly for large banks. We're just getting the people in place who are responsible for that. We should see that coming shortly. Obviously, that'll have impacts on capital ratios, but it'll go beyond that. It'll go into planning for these large banks.

We'll have more transparency around the stress tests. There can also be a cost-cutting opportunity there, in the sense that there's a lot of spending, a lot of costs that go involved with compliance. If those costs can come down, that'll be really beneficial for banks. So, I see, a lot of opportunity there, for example.

Oscar Pulido: And one of the other things that you touched on when we spoke in January was this opportunity in large cap value stocks that you thought this was an area that maybe had some performance embedded within it, and we've seen these stocks have a good run this year. Remind us again, when you say value stocks, what that means, and what is the role that they play in on certain markets like these?

Tony DeSpirito: So, in terms of value, I think about stocks that are statistically cheap on average, low PE, low price to book, et cetera. And obviously, as an active manager, how you implement that differs from other active managers. Price is what you pay, value is what you get. So, you really have to measure the value of a company correctly, and that's actually an active exercise.

Certainly, year-to-date, value stocks have done relatively well, but in the historical context, the outperformance of value is pretty small year-to-date. And so, it's not like you've missed the opportunity and value by any means. In terms of the opportunity, I think the biggest and easiest opportunity is just think about diversification and resiliency of your overall portfolio.

First of all, you've set certain targets of value and growth within your portfolio. Over the last several years, growth has outperformed, and that means it's become a larger percentage of your overall portfolio. So, there's an opportunity to get that back in line. So I think that will just add resiliency, will add diversification.

But also, it's not just portfolios, it's the indices themselves, the cap weight indices, in particular, if you think about the mega cap companies with a trillion dollar or more market cap, those all skew heavily towards tech and growth stocks. And so, as a result, the S&P 500, you think of it as a core index, but it's actually become more growthy in recent times.

And we've done work with our internal risk models to classify companies within the S&P 500 in terms of growth, core and value, and then we look at the different weightings in growth and value over time. And by the way, the index always skews a little bit growthy. But right now, it's even more skewed towards growth than it was at the peak of the dot.com bubble, believe it or not. So, what that tells you is you, if you think you own a core index, you actually own an index that's quite grow thy. And so, I think there's an opportunity to supplement that with value, which will create more resiliency. So, I think that's the real case for active within value.

And I think we saw the importance of having value in your portfolio year-to-date is a real diversifier. I’m particularly fond of mixing value and quality. When you think about a margin of safety, how do you get a margin of safety? One way you can do that is through quality. Companies that have more steady earning streams, companies that have healthy balance sheets, strong balance sheets, companies that pay dividends, right? Dividend paying stocks tend to hold up better in down markets. Certainly, this year was no different there.

The other way to create a margin of safety is through valuation. If you pay a low multiple, there's just less downside risk in general. Combining the two, it's really powerful and can be really a great way to create more resiliency in your whole portfolio.

Oscar Pulido: Tony, we talked about tariffs, and we've been talking about tariffs for a while now over the last couple of months. It's certainly not the only issue that investors or equity investors are thinking about. What are some of the other things that you think they should be thinking about or that you're thinking about for the outlook?

Tony DeSpirito: Well, without a doubt, over the long run, the most important thing to be watching is earnings because it's earnings that ultimately drive stock prices in the short run. It's going to be about valuations, it's going to be about factors, it's going to be about the news of the day. But in the long run, it's all about earnings and earnings power.

So, you always have to keep your eye on the prize, which is focus on earnings. Trade's not done. It's not completely off the table. There's still a lot of deals to be negotiated. There's a lot of pauses, and so stay tuned is what I'd say there. Obviously, the tax bill is going to be a huge deal over the next several months. And then, like I said, deregulation. So, there's still plenty to be watching, but again, the real focus should be on earnings.

Oscar Pulido: Another thing that comes to mind when we sat down in January, the US equity market had been the dominant outperformer for many years. And there was a lot of skepticism that markets outside the US could outperform. But what in fact we've seen this year is with some of the policy headlines coming out of the US and some of the uncertainty that's created, we have seen some outperformance from areas like Europe, for example. So, do you think those sorts of trends are going to continue? Are we going to continue to see outperformance from some of the international or ex-US markets?

Tony DeSpirito: Well, it's really hard to compare one market to another, it's like comparing apples and oranges. Without a doubt, the US is expensive relative to other markets around the globe, but on the other hand, it's also the highest quality. When you think about companies with a lot of innovation, when you think about high levels of profitability, return on invested capital, return of capital to shareholders through dividends, but also buybacks, and so yes, it's more expensive, but it also has more quality. I think it's a hard comparison to make. I tend to think more in terms of apples and oranges when you think about comparing markets.

That said, I will point out Japan is an interesting market for investors to look at. Japan has a very low correlation to other markets around the globe. Again, going back to this idea of resiliency and diversification, Japan's always a good diversifier for that reason. The US is interesting because it's the quality of the companies are so great. If you look at return on invested capital, across the globe, the US is about double the world average in terms of return on tangible capital. Japan's at the low end, but that's what makes it interesting is that there's an opportunity for that to improve.

Japanese corporations, they've really gotten religion with respect to this, both in terms of, improving profitability, simplifying their businesses, as well as returning capital to shareholders. And that has just been improving year after year in small increments, but it's added up. At the same time, the macro backdrop in Japan looks better. They've gone from multiple years of deflation to now having inflation, but you have this huge potential improvement in corporate profitability. And again, it's a real diversifier because it's so uncorrelated with the rest of the world. So, I do think that's an interesting market just to call out.

Finally, I prefer, and this is something we do in fundamental equities, it's more comparing apples to apples, so look within a global business, like energy or healthcare, and then make comparisons across the globe there and see where the opportunities set lies. Two areas where I think there's interesting opportunities in Europe that I would highlight - one's in energy and one's in pharmaceuticals.

If you look at, energy companies, in the US and in the UK and Europe, there's a pretty big valuation gap - 30- 40% - the US is more expensive. And if you look at the quality gap, historically there has been a big quality gap. There are instances of specific companies where they've been shrinking that quality gap over time. They've been becoming a lot more like the US, more focused on return on capital, et cetera. And so I think that makes for some interesting stock opportunities in the energy space to go abroad.

Similarly, in pharmaceuticals, the multiples are pretty close between the US and European pharmaceutical companies. The difference is quality is actually better in Europe in many cases. A lot of that goes to patent expirations. A lot of the US large companies have upcoming patent expirations that the Europeans just have further out in the future. Patent expirations are always tough because your revenue declines and then you have a certain fixity of costs that you have to deal with. So, I think that puts actually some of the US pharmaceutical companies at a disadvantage to some of the European ones.

And then, of course, healthcare, certainly in the policy hotspot at the moment. One of the ideas there is most favored nation status. Historically, drug prices in the US are a lot higher than they are in the rest of the developed world. One of the goals of the administration is to normalize that, that means lower drug prices in the U.S. but it also means maybe higher drug prices in Europe or other parts of the world.

And when you look at the global pharmaceutical companies, they are global businesses, but on average, the US ones tend to skew a little bit more towards the US; the European ones a little bit more towards Europe. If you think about drug prices normalizing around the world, with the US coming down, European prices going up, that's potentially an opportunity for some of these European companies. So in addition to just not having to deal with the patent expirations, they may have better pricing power over the next couple of years.

Oscar Pulido: So Tony, as we wrap up, I always enjoy hearing your words of wisdom for investors, and you've shared some of those already. You've told us that, we should focus on earnings. You said, price is what you pay, but value is what you get. What else would you say to investors who are trying to navigate the ups and downs of market volatility?

Tony DeSpirito: You know, Oscar, I think it's important and helpful to go back to first principles. And so, what are you doing when you're buying stocks? You're essentially buying a share of the world's productive capacity. And over time, through innovation, through company's abilities to do things faster, cheaper, better over time, that goes up. And that's a great place to be.

And yeah, there are setbacks along the way, temporary setbacks, that cause volatility, policy changes, inflation, recessions, even geopolitical conflicts, right? But the general direction is up. And when you think about volatility, sure, stock prices are going to be volatile, earnings less volatile. GDP, even less volatile than that. But the direction of travel is up for all of these things. And so, when you take that mindset, you really look at volatility differently, and you don't get as worried about short-term volatility, and you keep your eye on the long run. And so, one of my favorite expressions is that it's not about trying to time the market, it's about time in the market. And so if you can just extend your horizon, extend your thought process, and stay invested, that's going to be the key to wealth creation over time.

Oscar Pulido: And that actually rhymes with a message that I find myself telling people when they ask me about long-term investing, which is that when you invest in stocks, you're sort of betting on human ingenuity, and I think you used the word innovation. And if you believe that improves over time, then investing in stocks is potentially a rewarding experience over the long term. So, I guess I'm channeling my own inner Tony DiSpirito when I tell people that. Tony, it's always a pleasure to have you on, to hear about the equity markets and your outlook, and thank you for doing it here on The Bid.

Tony DeSpirito: I love doing it. Thank you, Oscar.

Sources: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2025, BlackRock; BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Morningstar & Bloomberg as of April 30, 2025;

This content is for informational purposes only and is not an offer or a solicitation. Reliance upon information in this material is at the sole discretion of the listener.

Reference to the names of each company mentioned in this communication is merely for explaining the investment strategy and should not be construed as investment advice or investment recommendation of those companies.

For full disclosures, go to Blackrock.com/corporate/compliance/bid-disclosures

MKTGSH0525U/M-4507498

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: FIBRA Macquarie's Occupancy Keeps Falling, But The Name Is Still Not Cheap
data.description:
FIBRA Macquarie (OTC:DBMBF) reported 1Q25 results at the end of April, with important comments about industrial real estate activity in April 2025, after tariff announcements.

The company's industrial occupancy continues to deteriorate, especially in some Northern markets like Reynosa and Monterrey. The comments from April are pretty bad, although they might be transitory.

The REIT trades at about 11x AFFO or 13x distributions guided for FY25. Considering the cycle position in the Mexican industrial market and the risks from the US, I don't think this is an attractive valuation. I maintain a Hold rating.

1Q25 results and comments on the market

Occupancy keeps falling: The most concerning data point of the earnings release is the fall in industrial occupancy, particularly in Northern markets where the company concentrates its holdings.

The aggregate industrial occupancy fell from 98.2% in 1Q24 to 94.7% in 1Q25 and from 95.8% in 4Q24. The trend is worsening since the election of Donald Trump.

The situation in some cities is particularly bad. When comparing occupancy by metro, from Supplementary Info of 1Q25 and 1Q24, we find that occupancy fell from 89% to 85% in Reynosa (3 million square feet), from 98.5% to 94.5% in Monterrey (6 million square feet), and from 100% to 93.5% in Ciudad Juarez (4.8 million square feet).

For example, the company added a 200 thousand square feet property in Apodaca (industrial outskirts of Monterrey) in 3Q24, and has not been able to lease it.

April has seen a drop in renewals: During the earnings call, management recognized that renewal activity fell significantly during April and after the tariffs announcements. Although the situation may have improved in May, these are not good cyclical indicators in general.

Renewal calendar is challenging: The company still has about 7% of its portfolio up for renewal this year, and around 20% in FY26. In general, the REIT's leases are exposed to a medium-term downcycle because the average lease length is 3.7 years, implying about 25% of leases come up for renewal each year.

Pricing still helps: Because of the renewal and adjustment mechanism, new leases are signed at higher prices, and existing leases move with inflation or fixed escalations. This makes the pricing per sq. ft. improve, and cushions the impact from the lower occupancy. This impact is still capturing some of the post-pandemic inflation push (imagine leases signed during 2020/2021/2022 at lower aggregate price levels) and a relatively healthy market. If occupancy keeps falling, and as inflation stabilizes, prices will stop carrying revenues, and margins will potentially fall.

This quarter, the company is able to maintain FFO and NOI figures in USD, but the guidance of increasing FFO by 1-5% seems hard to achieve if the situation doesn't improve in the industrial markets.

We have to remember that the company's EBITDA margin is close to 80%, and the company's FFO margin is close to 60%, meaning that each fall in revenue from a non-renewal hits the bottom line significantly.

Valuation is high for the cycle

If guidance is met, FIBRA Macquarie is expected to generate about $115-119 million in AFFO (EBITDA minus normalized maintenance CAPEX, interest, taxes, and some management costs and non-cash revenues), of which it plans to distribute about $95 million.

Trading at $1.3 billion on its ADR, and a little less on the Mexican exchange (MXN 24.6 billion ~ $1.28 billion), the company offers a distribution yield of 7.3% (or a P/Distribution of 13.7x). In addition, the company can invest $20 million in growth, or an additional 1.5% of yield.

In my opinion, when the US treasury bonds are yielding 5%, a 7.3% distribution yield, with low growth prospects in the mid-term, seems low.

The REIT should trade at a discount on distributions based on the position in the cycle (oversupplied industrial real estate in northern Mexico), and the risks coming from a potential US recession, and a potential tightening of trade or reduction of investment caused by trade uncertainty.

In my opinion, there will be better opportunities to buy FIBRA Macquarie down the road, and the yield today does not compensate for the higher uncertainty.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating on the name.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Uncertain U.S. Tariff Policy Raises Risks For Trading Partners
data.description:
The US trade and tariff vulnerability (TTV) index aims to assess the near-term vulnerability to reciprocal tariffs announced by the United States on April 2 and potential channels through which these new tariffs could affect the risk outlook.

Although tariffs are paused and subject to bilateral negotiations, significant uncertainty remains around the future trajectory of US tariff policy. There are potential repercussions for several liquidity and external income support factors in our short-term (ST) Country Default Risk (CDR) model.

Given the uncertainty surrounding near-term US trade and tariff policy toward the rest of the world, the TTV risk index creates a framework through which to measure and re-assess the impact US tariffs may have on our existing CDR (Country Default Risk) scores as tariff rates evolve, with a focus on the potential short-term CDR score impact.

All three of the US’s main trading partners (excluding the EU as a bloc), in order of US dollar value exports to the US, Canada, Mexico and China, fall into the “Highest” TTV risk class and in the top 20.

Of the 20 markets with the greatest TTV risk, seven are from Asia, with Hong Kong as 21st. They include China, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Malaysia, Vietnam and Cambodia. Apart from Cambodia, these are all prodigious exporters to the US, generating large recurring trade surpluses with the US, the key target for US tariff actions.

Among the 20 markets with significant exposure, three EU nations - Ireland, Slovakia, and Hungary - are also included, all of which are subject to the 20% tariff rate applied to the region.

Ireland is second most vulnerable in the global rankings, with a large trade surplus comprising mainly of pharmaceutical and organic chemical exports, typically by US foreign direct investment in the market, also taking advantage of low Irish corporate taxes and free trade in the very large EU market with no exchange rate risks in the exports to eurozone.

Switzerland appears after Ireland in the top 20 with its predominant pharmaceutical exports, followed by Germany and Slovakia as the next most TTV risk after Ireland in the EU, largely due to their automotive, machinery and chemical exports to the US. Slovakia harbors several German automotive manufacturers geared for export.

Just over half of all 199 markets fall into our designated “Modest” or lowest TTV risk class. Most African, Latin American and Caribbean markets fall into this risk class. Only one G7 market appears in this class: the United Kingdom. Other medium-sized economies in this TTV risk class, indicative of moderate risk, include Brazil, Spain, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Australia and New Zealand.

One aim of the TTV risk index is to use the results as a guide to possible scoring and outlook changes to our short-term CDR risk scores (ST-CDR). We can identify three distinct risk transmission channels from US tariffs onto our ST-CDR risk criteria, two market based (capital and credit) and one based on trade.

Capital markets channel

Global capital flows, both equity and bond related (or “portfolio”), are the most sensitive and fleet-footed to changes in the risk environment. We are already seeing some tentative consequences from the announcement of new US tariffs in early April.

The general risk environment for emerging and frontier markets is elevated; specifically, a net outflow of portfolio funds from mainland China and others in Asia becoming evident. Ordinarily, safe haven flows benefit the US where the US treasury market is the largest, deepest and most liquid.

However, on this occasion there have been some outflows from US treasuries, possibly reflecting repatriation flows. Many of the higher US tariff rates imposed are concentrated in Asia, where many export-dependent economies have recurring trade surpluses with the US.

Another tendency in heightened global risk environments is the return of “home biases,” where foreign portfolio capital tends to return home to their original capital sources or savings base.

The actual criteria that can be affected on the ST-CDR model are the interest rate and risk spread-based criteria. Foreign exchange reserves may also be marginally affected. Possible changes in the risk scores on these ST-CDR criteria here may reflect much weaker foreign exchange earnings if exports are squeezed by tariffs, and as denominators or debt service supports in these credit risk indicators, or higher interest or risk spreads as higher debt service burdens in their numerators.

Credit markets channel

Traditional structured credits, like trade and project finance, still form an important part of global financial flows, particularly for frontier markets. Where global banks perceive their markets as under heightened risk, many will be reviewing their entire dollar limits on the bank’s global balance sheet, not just their overall dollar value exposures, but with an initial tendency to shorten the maturities in their exposures as a possible next step to exit the market.

This credit retrenchment process would involve a non-rollover of short-term debt, that is, credit liquidation, before exiting the market through the foreign exchange market door.

In terms of impact on the ST-CDR model, this would involve downward pressure on the foreign exchange position to the extent there is credit and capital flight. The tell-tale associated signal here could also be exchange-rate depreciation.

Our “external liquidity gap” criterion in the ST-CDR model is a version of the “net short position,” involving the current account balance itself and levels of short-term debt that may rise initially as global banks “go short” on a market, and posing an increasing risk to the foreign exchange liquidity position if liquidated.

Trade channel

The clearest direct vulnerability from US tariffs on exports in the trade balance is incorporated into our external current account balance criterion. If US tariffs slow export growth or limit exports, the implication is that the market’s trade and current account surplus with the US is narrowed, or deficit deepened. This tendency may have a direct impact on our current account balance criterion in the ST-CDR model.

Furthermore, if new US tariffs restrain a market’s exports and export growth, this could directly affect its foreign exchange earnings capacity. Total foreign exchange earnings is a key support (denominator) to several solvency related credit risk indicators in our ST-CDR and MT-CDR models.

Caveats

The main caveat to the TTV score results and rankings is that there is currently a 90-day reprieve from April 10 until the new proposed tariffs. As a result, the TTV index scores and rankings should be viewed tentatively and as purely illustrative while the situation remains in flux, subject to bilateral negotiations, and are not to be seen as forecasts or probabilities, but something around which new risk conditions could crystalize in the very near term.

Secondly, the TTV index essentially reflects the intentions behind the new US tariff policy, targeting those economies with which it has a trade deficit to larger or lesser extents. The TTV index presented here aims to sharpen and deepen the uneven tariff risk impact across markets, building on the headline percentage tariff alone.

There is no direct or proportional impact from the TTV index onto our ST-CDR scoring model, only indirectly through the channels described above. Economies will be variously dispositioned from their ST-CDR risk circumstance to the impact from any TTV index influence.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Central Europe's Big Banks Sharpen Focus On Risk Costs Amid Tariff Hurdles
data.description:
'Interesting times ahead'

Austria-based Raiffeisen Bank International AG (RBI) (OTCPK:RAIFF) (OTCPK:RAIFY) is preparing for the potential fallout from trade disruptions, having launched an internal stress test of its loan book to assess tariff sensitivity.

"I'm satisfied that we are well prepared for interesting times ahead," Chief Risk Officer Hannes Mösenbacher said during a May 6 earnings call.

RBI booked €71 million of additional management overlays in the first quarter for its core business, excluding Russia. Overlays are additional reserves that cover potential losses from novel risks that are beyond the scope of typical credit risk models.

This was primarily due to heightened economic uncertainty, even as credit risk in its core business during the period was "very benign," according to Mösenbacher.RBI may recognize further overlays in the second quarter, the executive said.

"The macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty also leads us to leaving the risk cost guidance at the moment unchanged [at] up to 50 basis points," Mösenbacher said.

Société Générale SA's (OTCPK:SCGLF) (OTCPK:SCGLY) Czech unit, Komercní banka a.s., booked a temporary reserve of roughly 500 million koruny in the first quarter, reflecting the potential impact of the trade tensions. The reserve was estimated based on a projected increase of 25 bps in the probability of default for the corporate segment, and will be reevaluated once the bank updates its macroeconomic scenarios later in May.

Despite the reserve, Komercní banka improved its risk outlook for the year, projecting it to fall on the lower end of the range of 0 bps to 10 bps, significantly below its through-the-cycle range of 20 bps to 30 bps.

Komercní banka was the only lender in a sample of 10 major banks in Central Europe with a negative cost of risk - measured as annualized loan loss provisions as a proportion of average gross customer loans - in the first quarter due to a net release of provisions, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. KBC Group NV's (OTCPK:KBCSF) (OTCPK:KBCSY) Czech unit Ceskoslovenská obchodní banka a. s., RBI and fellow Austrian lender Erste Group Bank AG (OTCPK:EBKOF) (OTCPK:EBKDY) also had some of the lowest risk costs in the sample.

Similar to RBI, Erste reaffirmed its risk cost guidance for the year.

"Despite this very good start to the year, we decided to remain prudent and confirm our 2025 risk cost guidance unchanged at about 25 basis points, not least because there's a lot of uncertainty out there that impairs visibility. … Should these concerns fade away as the year passes and our credit risk performance stays healthy, we will certainly revisit the outlook," Erste Chief Risk Officer Alexandra Habeler-Drabek said in an April 30 earnings call.

Commerzbank AG's (OTCPK:CRZBF) (OTCPK:CRZBY) Polish unit, mBank SA, also confirmed its 2025 cost of risk outlook at between 70 bps and 80 bps, with its executives noting April 30 that currently there are no negative implications for its corporate clients from the heightened trade tensions.

mBank (OTCPK:BREJY) (OTCPK:MBAKF), along with Polish lenders Santander Bank Polska SA (OTCPK:BKZHF) and ING Bank Slaski SA (ING), had the highest first quarter costs of risk in the sample.

Lending outlook

Erste, RBI and Hungary-based OTP Bank Nyrt (OTCPK:OTPGF) (OTCPK:OTPBF) reaffirmed their loan growth outlooks for the year. Erste is anticipating loan growth of about 5%, while RBI expects to expand its portfolios by 6%-7%, excluding Russia. OTP Bank anticipates foreign currency-adjusted organic performing loan growth above the 9% reported in 2024.

Poland's largest lender, PKO Bank Polski SA, also expects loan growth to continue this year, supported by a recent 50 bps interest rate cut by the Polish central bank. PKO Bank is optimistic that negotiations between the US and its trading partners will yield constructive outcomes, which could positively influence both the global and Polish economies, thereby enhancing lending prospects throughout 2025.

mBank expects its corporate and retail loan portfolios to outpace market growth, although it acknowledged a downside risk from US tariffs in its first quarter earnings presentation.

Almost all banks in the sample demonstrated year-over-year loan growth in the first quarter. Most also exhibited quarter-over-quarter lending growth, with Komercní banka and Poland-based Bank Polska Kasa Opieki SA (OTCPK:BKPKF) being the two exceptions.

Supported by loan growth, all banks in the sample demonstrated year-over-year growth in first quarter net interest income (NII).

PKO Bank recorded the highest year-over-year NII growth, surpassing 15%. OTP Bank followed with an 11.3% growth. OTP Bank was also the only institution in the sample to demonstrate quarterly growth.

Erste reported the lowest year-over-year growth and the highest quarterly decline, with its NII up 1.1% from the first quarter of 2024 and down 3.4% from the linked quarter.
